flashcards.back: The most common Bitcoin transaction type. Funds are locked to the hash of the recipient's public key. To spend, the recipient provides the matching public key and a valid signature.
A transaction requiring M-of-N signatures to authorize spending (e.g., 2-of-3). Used for shared wallets, escrow services, and enhanced security where no single party has unilateral control.

A transaction type that locks funds to the hash of a script (redeem script). Enables complex conditions like multisig while keeping addresses simple. The spender reveals the full script when spending.
The first transaction in every block. Has no inputs, creates new cryptocurrency as the block reward, and collects all transaction fees. This is how new bitcoin enters circulation.
Segregated Witness moves signature (witness) data outside the main transaction structure. This fixes transaction malleability, reduces effective size, and allows more transactions per block.
A transaction that cannot be spent until a specified time or block height. Uses locktime field or CLTV/CSV opcodes. Enables payment channels, escrow, and inheritance planning.

flashcards.front: How do Ethereum transaction types differ from Bitcoin?
flashcards.back: Ethereum has: 1) Simple value transfers (ETH between accounts), 2) Contract creation transactions (deploying new smart contracts), 3) Message call transactions (calling functions on existing contracts).

flashcards.front: What is a Hashed Timelock Contract (HTLC)?
flashcards.back: A transaction type combining a hash lock and a time lock. Used in atomic cross-chain swaps and Lightning Network. Funds are released if the recipient provides a secret before a deadline.
A transaction output that stores arbitrary data (up to 80 bytes) on the blockchain. The output is provably unspendable. Used for timestamping, proof of existence, and metadata storage.
A single transaction with multiple outputs, sending bitcoin to several recipients at once. More efficient than separate transactions because it saves on transaction overhead and fees.
